description: Serverless runtime for JS/TS workers built on Bun/JSC, with isolated GlobalObjects per worker, TenantManager for routing, and a POSIX HTTP server — invoked via `bun serverless`

userStories.title: Integrate C++ files into Bun's build system
userStories.description: As a developer, I need the new C++ files in src/serverless/ to be compiled and linked correctly in the Bun fork build, so that `bun serverless` is available after building.
acceptanceCriteria: The .cpp files from src/serverless/ are added to the compiled sources list in the fork's build.zig
serverless_command.zig compiles alongside other src/cli/ files without modifying the existing directory structure
The function `extern "C" int bun_serverless_main(const char* config_path, int port)` exported by serverless_bootstrap.cpp is linked correctly and visible to Zig via `extern fn`
`bun build` (or the fork's equivalent build command) completes without errors after the changes
The resulting binary responds to `bun serverless --help` without symbol errors
Build and link succeed

userStories.title: Parse workers config file (workers.json)
userStories.description: As a developer, I want to define my workers in a config file so the runtime knows which JS/TS files serve which routes.
acceptanceCriteria: The runtime accepts a workers.json file with format: { "workers": [{ "name": string, "route": string, "script": string }] }
Required fields: name, route, script. Missing field causes startup failure with error: Worker entry missing required field: "<field>"
Duplicate worker name causes startup failure with error: Duplicate worker name: "<name>"
The script field is resolved relative to the config file directory, not the process cwd
Clear error on startup if config file doesn't exist or has invalid JSON
Clear error if a referenced script file doesn't exist (after path resolution)
Build and link succeed

userStories.title: Validate technical assumptions — GlobalObject, ESM, JS invocation
userStories.description: As a runtime, I need to validate that the riskiest technical core works before building the full HTTP pipeline. This is the highest-risk step of the PoC.
acceptanceCriteria: Instantiate a dedicated Zig::GlobalObject inside a shared JSC::VM
Load a trivial ESM module (export default { fetch() { return new Response("ok") } }) in that GlobalObject's context
Extract the default export and verify it's an object with a callable fetch property
Invoke the fetch function from the default export with simple JS arguments and get a valid return (no HTTP pipeline)
Validate that `import { readFile } from "node:fs/promises"` works inside a module loaded in this context
Validate that the Promise returned by the fetch handler resolves correctly via JSC's event loop/microtask queue
All validation can be done via a standalone test or temporary main — no HttpServer dependency
The validation produces a standalone executable (e.g. src/serverless/validate_assumptions) that prints one line per validated hypothesis: checkmark + description
If any hypothesis fails, the executable prints X + failure description and exits with code 1
This artifact is the definitive pass/fail criterion for US-002
Build and link succeed
userStories.notes: GATE: If this fails, the PoC approach needs to be reevaluated. Do not proceed to US-005 until this passes.

userStories.title: Implement TenantManager and JsRuntime with separated responsibilities
userStories.description: As a runtime, I need two components with clearly separated responsibilities: JsRuntime handles everything JSC-related; TenantManager handles worker registry, lifecycle, and routing.
acceptanceCriteria: JsRuntime::init() creates a single JSC::VM instance for the process
JsRuntime::createWorker(scriptPath) creates a Zig::GlobalObject, loads script as ESM, extracts default export, returns opaque WorkerHandle*
JsRuntime::callFetch(handle, ...) acquires JSC lock, invokes fetch handler, awaits Promise, extracts status/headers/body, returns FetchResult (see FR-5)
JsRuntime::destroyWorker(handle) unprotects GlobalObject from GC and frees resources
JsRuntime::deinit() destroys all handles and the VM. Only place that destroys the VM
JsRuntime has no knowledge of route names, worker names, or routing logic
TenantManager::init(JsRuntime* runtime) initializes with reference to JsRuntime
TenantManager::loadWorker(name, route, scriptPath) calls runtime->createWorker(), stores Worker{ name, route, handle } in unordered_map
TenantManager::route(path) resolves which worker handles the path (exact match first, then longest prefix match for routes ending in /*)
TenantManager::deinit() iterates all workers and calls runtime->destroyWorker() for each
TenantManager does not include any JSC headers and does not call any JSC API directly
Build and link succeed

userStories.title: Initialize one GlobalObject per worker on startup
userStories.description: As a runtime, I need to create a dedicated JSC::GlobalObject for each worker defined in the config, to ensure state isolation between workers.
acceptanceCriteria: For each entry in workers.json, JsRuntime creates a separate Zig::GlobalObject
All GlobalObjects share the same JSC::VM (created once during JsRuntime init)
The worker's script is loaded as an ES Module in the corresponding GlobalObject's context via JSC's module loader (not JSC::evaluate())
After module loading, JsRuntime extracts and stores the default export ({ fetch }) for use on each request
Failure to load a script (syntax error, invalid import, etc.) displays error and halts startup
The GlobalObject is protected from GC while the worker is active
Build and link succeed

userStories.title: Define worker convention — export default { fetch } + Node.js APIs
userStories.description: As a worker developer, I want a single clear convention for writing workers, with access to Node.js APIs already implemented by Bun.
acceptanceCriteria: The worker exports `export default` with an object containing a fetch method
The runtime accesses the default export after ESM loading and verifies it's an object with a callable fetch property
If default export doesn't exist or fetch is not a function, runtime fails on startup with descriptive error (not per-request)
Inside the worker, these APIs are available via Bun's GlobalObject: fetch() global, crypto (Web Crypto), URL, URLSearchParams, TextEncoder, TextDecoder, Node.js APIs via static import
The second argument env passed to fetch is an empty JS object {} (placeholder for future compatibility)
Exporting only `export function fetch(...)` without `export default` results in startup error: Worker "name": default export must be an object with a fetch method
Build and link succeed

userStories.title: Integrate with Bun CLI — `bun serverless` subcommand
userStories.description: As a developer, I want to start the serverless runtime via `bun serverless` as a native CLI subcommand.
acceptanceCriteria: New subcommand 'serverless' registered in Bun's main CLI dispatcher following existing subcommand patterns (enum tag + root command matcher)
Command file created (e.g. src/cli/serverless_command.zig) with exec function that parses --config <path> (default: ./workers.json) and --port <number> (default: 3000)
Accepts positional shortcut: `bun serverless ./workers.json` equals `--config ./workers.json`
Calls the serverless runtime bootstrap via extern C function: bun_serverless_main(config_path, port)
On error, prints clear message to stderr and returns exit code 1
On success, starts HTTP server and keeps process alive until SIGINT
`bun serverless --help` prints basic usage with options and examples
Typecheck passes (Zig component)

userStories.title: Implement internal runtime bootstrap (serverless_bootstrap.cpp)
userStories.description: As a runtime, I need an internal C++ entry point that orchestrates the complete startup: parse config, init JsRuntime, load workers via TenantManager, start HttpServer.
acceptanceCriteria: serverless_bootstrap.cpp exports `extern "C" int bun_serverless_main(const char* config_path, int port)` as bootstrap function
Bootstrap executes sequence: Config::load() -> JsRuntime::init() -> TenantManager loads workers -> HttpServer::listen()
On startup, prints to stdout: [bun-serverless] Loaded N workers, worker list with routes, and Listening on http://localhost:<port>
Registers SIGINT handler for graceful shutdown: destroys all workers via TenantManager::deinit() and exits
Returns 0 on success (after graceful shutdown), or 1 on startup error
Build and link succeed

userStories.title: HTTP server that routes requests to workers
userStories.description: As a developer, I want the runtime to start an HTTP server that automatically directs each request to the correct worker based on configured routes.
acceptanceCriteria: Runtime starts HTTP server on configured port (default: 3000, overridden via --port or PORT env)
Path /_metrics is intercepted by HttpServer before routing and responded directly (see US-009)
Each HTTP request is routed via TenantManager::route(path) using the algorithm defined in FR-4
If no worker matches, returns 404 Not Found with body {"error":"No worker found for route"}
Runtime constructs a JS Request object (Web API) with method, full url, headers, and body (read entirely into memory)
Runtime calls JsRuntime::callFetch(handle, ...) to invoke the handler and get a FetchResult
FetchResult (status, headers, body) is serialized as HTTP response on the socket
If worker throws uncaught exception, returns 500 with body {"error":"<exception message>"}
If fetch handler returns a non-Response value, returns 500 with body {"error":"Worker returned a non-Response value"}
HTTP parser limits: HTTP/1.1 only, no keep-alive, 4MB max body (413 for larger), no chunked transfer, max 64 headers
Build and link succeed

userStories.title: Basic metrics endpoint (/_metrics)
userStories.description: As an operator, I want a GET /_metrics endpoint to see worker status and shared VM memory usage.
acceptanceCriteria: GET /_metrics returns JSON with vm_heap_size_bytes, vm_heap_capacity_bytes, and workers array (name + route per worker)
vm_heap_size_bytes represents current total heap size of the shared VM
vm_heap_capacity_bytes represents total allocated heap capacity of the VM
Workers list is generated by TenantManager — contains only name and route, no per-worker memory field
Endpoint is intercepted and responded by HttpServer before routing to workers
GET /_metrics returns 200 OK
Any other HTTP method on /_metrics returns 405 Method Not Allowed
Build and link succeed

userStories.title: Example workers and end-to-end validation
userStories.description: As a developer, I want example workers and a workers.json ready to validate the runtime end-to-end with `bun serverless`.
acceptanceCriteria: examples/workers.json contains at least two workers (hello and api)
examples/workers/hello.ts returns a simple text response
examples/workers/api.ts returns JSON and uses new URL() for internal routing
End-to-end validation via: bun serverless --config ./examples/workers.json --port 3000
curl http://localhost:3000/hello returns 200 OK with hello worker body
curl http://localhost:3000/api/health returns 200 OK with {"status":"ok"}
curl http://localhost:3000/nonexistent returns 404 with {"error":"No worker found for route"}
curl http://localhost:3000/_metrics returns valid JSON with VM heap metrics and loaded workers list
